data.title: U.S. auto retail: Sonic, Asbury, and Group 1 see downgrades at JPMorgan; Cars.com is upgraded
data.description:
JPMorgan issued a sweeping preview of the U.S. auto retail sector ahead of the Q2 earnings season. Analyst Rajat Gupta said the overall risk-reward profile of the sector is negative after share price outperformance amidst weakening fundamentals. Gupta warned that there is potential for deceleration in EPS growth as supply exceeds demand. The firm issued three downgrades (ABG, GPI, SAH) and one upgrade.

Cars.com (CAR) (upgraded to Overweight from Neutral): "There are signs of improvement in product development and adoption, along with increased optionality following the recent expansion into digital D2D wholesale through the acquisition of DealerClub."

Lithia Motors (NYSE:LAD) (Overweight rating): "LAD is now the only OW rated name in our coverage given room to disconnect favorably on EPS/EBITDA growth from adjacent drivers like DFC, and relatively higher balance sheet optionality with a greater emphasis on buybacks. 

Group 1 Automotive (NYSE:GPI) (downgraded to Neutral from Overweight): "We expect consistent superior execution, though see outsized revision benefits as fading in the medium-term as Inchcape cost out actions are in the rear-view, UK faces a tough backdrop, and there are minimal adjacent earnings drivers."

AutoNation (Neutral): "Shares have outperformed YTD on the back of relatively cleaner revisions, with AN Finance turning profitable sooner than anticipated. The company is also a relativebeneficiary of tariffs given their brand mix, though as an offset, is likely to see mixed fundamentals from outsized exposure to Florida where housing prices are under pressure. That said, like peers they are not immune to industry pressures broadly, and we believe AN Finance inflection is still a couple of years away, as NIMs normalize to current origination spreads, to drive earnings differentiation."

Asbury Automotive Group (NYSE:ABG) (downgraded to Underweight from Neutral): "The company is likely to face a subdued relative revision backdrop, given continued weak SS performance, and minimal balance sheet levers (~3.5x net debt/EBITDA pro-forma for the Herb Chambers acquisition). Brand mix is favorable in context of tariffs, though there remains continued pressure at Stellantis (volumes down ~11% y/y in 2Q vs +2% for industry) and also relatively weaker regional mix (D.C, Florida). "

Sonic Automotive (NYSE:SAH) (downgraded to Underweight from Overweight): "After a period of strong outperformance (~2,600 bps YTD and ~3,000bps last 12 months), largely deserved as franchise execution remains above average and EchoPark has demonstrated a strong return to profitable growth, we believe shares are due for a pull back given relatively unfavorable exposure to brands impacted by auto tariffs, potential for affordability headwinds slowing EchoPark recovery, and tougher comps in P&S in 2H/early 2026."

Penske Automotive Group (NYSE:PAG) (Underweight rating): "While management execution has remained among best-in-class, we continue to see fundamentals as tough given unfavorable exposure to tariff-exposed brands, a tough UK backdrop, and continued weakness in freight markets. While the relative multiple has compressed from the peaks of 2022/2023, we look for a better entry point and/or signs of a turn in the non-auto businesses to get constructive."

data.title: KKR invests in e-commerce platform SupplyHouse, terms undisclosed
data.description:
Investment firm KKR & Co. (NYSE:KKR) has invested in the e-commerce platform for the distribution of HVAC, plumbing, and electrical products, SupplyHouse.

The financial terms of the strategic partnership were not disclosed.

KKR is making its investment through Ascendant Strategy, which invests in middle market businesses.

Shares were -0.12% pre-market to $142.88.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: U.S. Bancorp beats Q2 profit estimates, issues Q3 outlook
US-UAE data center deal using Nvidia chips slowed down amid security woes - report
data.description:
Some officials from the Trump administration are holding up efforts to finalize a deal that would let the United Arab Emirates buy billions of dollars of Nvidia’s AI chips due to national-security concerns, The Wall Street Journal reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter.

President Donald Trump announced the tentative agreement during his visit to the Middle East in May, and the sides hoped to work out the details quickly. However, the two countries have not been able to hammer out the specifics of the deal to date, partly because some U.S. officials have expressed concerns that China could get access to the advanced U.S. technology, the report added.

The disagreement may not be resolved unless the U.A.E. agrees to different terms to address the U.S. officials’ concerns related to national-security, according to the report.

The officials have discussed, for example, cutting off direct access to chips by Abu Dhabi-based AI startup G4, which was expected to get access to about 20% of the chips as part of the deal.

Now, the Commerce Department does not intend to approve chips going to G42, though it may in the future, the report added.

Nvidia, the U.S. Department of Commerce, G42, OpenAI and Microsoft did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha. 

Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick “is confident that the implementation plan for the deal signed in the Emirates will continue on time and on schedule,” a spokesman said, the report noted. 

U.A.E. officials have also shown optimism about the discussions. The deal “will deliver enormous benefits to both countries,” the U.A.E.’s ambassador to the U.S., Yousef Al Otaiba, said in a statement, the report added.

On Tuesday, Lutnick and other U.S. officials met with representatives from the U.A.E. at a tech and energy event in Pittsburgh and talked about how to move the agreement forward, as per the report.

However, the slow speed has pitted administration officials against each other and frustrated some tech-industry executives looking to ramp up their AI businesses overseas, according to the report.

Nvidia's CEO told President Donald Trump about the importance of the deal during a recent meeting, the report stated.

Some administration officials are concerned that Chinese tech giant Huawei Technologies, which has been trying to sell chips in the Middle East, could capitalize on the delay.

At the heart of the matter related to the holdup are some of the most advanced semiconductors available, chips from Nvidia that would power AI data centers.

Last month also it was reported that the multi-billion dollar deal to build data centers for AI, Stargate UAE, using U.S. technology is far from being completed due to continuous concerns related to security.

Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), Oracle (NYSE:ORCL), OpenAI, Cisco Systems (NASDAQ:CSCO), SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY) (OTCPK:SFTBF) and G42 are collaborating to build Stargate UAE, plans for which were announced in May following Trump's visit to the country.

OpenAI and G42 are both backed by Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT).

Under the tentative terms, the U.A.E. would get hundreds of thousands of Nvidia chips over years and help build data centers. Most of the chips would go to U.S. tech companies operating the data centers.

OpenAI and Microsoft are among the companies which are expected to operate data centers in the U.A.E. To have chips exported to the U.A.E., the companies would require licenses that would likely have to be approved by several U.S. departments, a process that could increase scrutiny of the national-security implications, the WSJ report noted. 

Besides getting earmarked for 20% of the chips in the tentative deal, G42 could also have some involvement in the U.A.E. data centers operated by U.S. companies, the report added.

Some U.S. officials have shown concerns that China could find ways to access the advanced chips via G42 or other U.A.E. entities and personnel involved in the project, the report noted.

Changing the terms to no longer send chips directly to G42 could anger the U.A.E., which sees the deal as important to keeping pace in AI and saw G42’s involvement as a central part of the framework agreed to in May, according to the report.

“If we don’t provide the technology, then our global competitors will,” said White House AI czar David Sacks at the summit in Pittsburgh, adding that concern about chips being diverted “has been wildly blown out of proportion.” 

Sacks was among the lead architects of the U.S.-U.A.E. chip agreement, while Lutnick helped close the agreement. Officials from the two countries who were supposed to move the chip deal forward set the details have barely met since the deal was signed, the report added.

data.title: Farm-to-fork commerce platform Local Line is a notable holding in Chipotle's venture investment portfolio
data.description:
Chipotle's (NYSE:CMG) Cultivate Next venture fund was launched in 2022 to support companies that align with its mission of building a better food system, particularly those that improve food access, labor practices, and sustainability across the supply chain. 

One of the fund's notable investments is in Local Line, which is a privately held Canadian company founded in 2015 and headquartered in Kitchener, Ontario. It operates as an e-commerce and logistics platform designed specifically for farmers, food hubs, cooperatives, and local food suppliers.

Since Cultivate Next announced its investment in Local Line in 2023, Local Line has expanded to 14 countries, onboarded national restaurant chains, grocers, and distributors, and helped eliminate 940 million food miles by directly connecting buyers to local farms. In 2023, Local Line acquired Forager, a Maine-based online marketplace connecting local farms with grocers, expanding Local Line’s customer base and capabilities in grocery procurement for local foods. In 2024, Local Line partnered with Freight Farms to provide exclusive platform benefits to container farming operations, helping more new farmers launch successful online businesse

The investment in Local Line is part of Chipotle's (NYSE:CMG) long-running Local Grower Support Program, which enabled the fast-casual chain to source over 47 million pounds of local produce in 2024 alone. Partnering with Local Line allowed Chipotle (CMG) to upgrade from assorted supply chain tools to a centralized platform that simplifies ordering, documentation, and distribution for both its procurement team and its farm partners. 

The partnership also powered the launch of Chipotle's (CMG) new local farm grant program, which provided support to 100 small and family-owned farms in 2024. As Local Line scales globally, Chipotle (CMG) sees the collaboration as a key step in building a more resilient, sustainable, and transparent food ecosystem.

Chipotle's (CMG) Cultivate Next venture fund portfolio also includes Brassica, CH4 Global, GreenField Robotics, Hyphen, Local Line, Lumachain, Meati Foods, Nitricity, Plantible, Vebu, and Zero Acre Farms.

data.title: GrabAGun, backed by Donald Trump Jr., sinks in trading debut
data.description:
Online firearms retailer GrabAGun (NYSE:PEW), whose board includes Donald Trump Jr., sank nearly 24% in its trading debut on Wednesday.

The firm went public on the New York Stock Exchange after merging with special purpose acquisition company Colombier II (NYSE:CLBR). GrabAGun (NYSE:PEW) raised $179M in gross proceeds from the merger, or over $119M after deducting expenses.

The proceeds will be used to for working capital and other purposes to accelerate the company's future growth initiatives, acquisition plans and other general expenses.

Trump Jr. was projected to own 300,000 GrabAGun (PEW) shares, or about 1% of the company's stock, according to a prospectus filed last month.

"To be able to come back to the NYSE and actually take a gun company public feels like such a vindication of all the insanity, all of the 'woke' nonsense that we've been watching and facing for the last decade in America," he told Fox Business. 

Trump Jr. also serves as an adviser to drone maker Unusual Machines and is a director on the board of online marketplace PublicSquare, which bills itself as "anti-woke."

data.title: The Travelers Companies Non-GAAP EPS of $6.51 beats by $2.90
data.description:
• The Travelers Companies press release (NYSE:TRV): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $6.51 beats by $2.90.
  • Net written premiums of $11.54B (+3.8% Y/Y) beats by $520M.
  • Consolidated combined ratio improved 9.9 points from the prior year quarter to a very strong 90.3%.
  • Underlying combined ratio improved 3.0 points from the prior year quarter to an excellent 84.7%.
  • Catastrophe losses were $927 million pre-tax compared to $1.509 billion in the prior year quarter.
